coredata.dc:title: Influence of bonding variance on electron affinity in graphene quantum dot-barium titanate nanocomposites for drug delivery system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Jung-Hua
Chen, Li
Cho, Er-Chieh
Lee, Kuen-Chan
coredata.dc:description: Although chemotherapy remains a prevalent option in cancer treatment, its adverse effects on normal cells and suboptimal pharmacokinetics often limits its effectiveness. To address these challenges, this study successfully developed a new multifunctional drug delivery system comprising a covalent composite of graphene quantum dots and barium titanate nanoparticles. Notably, despite numerous reports on the surface modification of graphene quantum dots, studies focusing on cancer cell inhibition via different covalent bonds are scarce. To bridge this gap, this system was synthesized using eco-friendly esterification and amidation pathways. The anticancer drug doxorubicin was employed as a model drug, and hyaluronic acid was used to encapsulate the delivery system, enhancing its sustained release capabilities. Comprehensive material characterization confirmed the successful synthesis of the system. Its high drug loading capacity and acid-sensitive release can be attributed to the unique structure of the graphene quantum dots. Subsequent in vitro and in vivo biological evaluations not only demonstrated the system’s remarkable cancer inhibition efficacy but also accentuated the distinct impacts of the two bonding types. The underlying mechanism is believed to involve bonding affinity and electron transfer, findings that are corroborated by the experimental data. Additionally, results from animal models provide clear evidence for the potential application of this system (HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs) in cancer therapeutics and imaging. In conclusion, this research elucidates the variances in drug carrier efficacy based on different covalent bond modifications for cancer treatment and introduces a novel drug delivery system that synergistically combines imaging and targeting capabilities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Barium titanate
Esterification bonding
Amidation bonding
Drug delivery system

Synthesis of GQD@BTNPs composites via amidation
In vitro drug loading and releasing
In vivo xenograft animal model experiment.

We also appreciate the supports from the Core Facility Center and the Animal Center at Taipei Medical University and National Taipei University of Education (NTUE), Taiwan, for the instrumental analysis and technical support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs. (a) XRD pattern of BTNPs and GQD@BTNPs; (b) UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of BTNPs and GQD@BTNPs; (c) FT-IR spectrum of BTNPs and GQD@BTNPs; (d) ζ-potential in every process of modification of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs.
The TGA of GQD@BTNPs(amide) and GQD@BTNPs(ester).
EDS diagram of (a) BTNPs, (b) GQD@BTNPs(ester) and (c) GQD@BTNPs(amide); Table 1. Element proportion of BTNPs, GQD@BTNPs(ester) and GQD@BTNPs(amide).
TEM images of (a) GQD@BTNPs(amide), (b) GQD@BTNPs(ester), (c) HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(amide) and (d) HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(ester), (e) HR-TEM of GQD@BTNPs(amide) and (f) DLS measurements.
In vitro release profile of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(amide) and HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(ester) at pH = 7 and 5.5.

Cell viability of no treatment (Control), DOX, HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(amide) and HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(ester) after (a) 24 hr, (b) 48 hr and (c) 72 hr incubation; Table 3. Comparison of IC50 after 72 hr incubation.

Antitumor efficacy of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(amide) and HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(ester) in vivo. (a) Time-dependent relative tumor volume (n = 5) *p < 0.05; (b) Tumor visual images after treatment of 35 days; (C) Body weight changes.
(a) Modification and conjugation protocol of the HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs; (b) utilization of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs for cancer cell inhibition and difference between amide group and ester group.
Summary of drug loading efficiency (DLE) and drug loading capacity (DLC).

head.ce:title: Influence of bonding variance on electron affinity in graphene quantum dot-barium titanate nanocomposites for drug delivery system

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme. Modification and conjugation protocol of the HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs and the utilization of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs for cancer cell inhibition and difference between amide group and ester group.

Although chemotherapy remains a prevalent option in cancer treatment, its adverse effects on normal cells and suboptimal pharmacokinetics often limits its effectiveness. To address these challenges, this study successfully developed a new multifunctional drug delivery system comprising a covalent composite of graphene quantum dots and barium titanate nanoparticles. Notably, despite numerous reports on the surface modification of graphene quantum dots, studies focusing on cancer cell inhibition via different covalent bonds are scarce. To bridge this gap, this system was synthesized using eco-friendly esterification and amidation pathways. The anticancer drug doxorubicin was employed as a model drug, and hyaluronic acid was used to encapsulate the delivery system, enhancing its sustained release capabilities. Comprehensive material characterization confirmed the successful synthesis of the system. Its high drug loading capacity and acid-sensitive release can be attributed to the unique structure of the graphene quantum dots. Subsequent in vitro and in vivo biological evaluations not only demonstrated the system’s remarkable cancer inhibition efficacy but also accentuated the distinct impacts of the two bonding types. The underlying mechanism is believed to involve bonding affinity and electron transfer, findings that are corroborated by the experimental data. Additionally, results from animal models provide clear evidence for the potential application of this system (HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs) in cancer therapeutics and imaging. In conclusion, this research elucidates the variances in drug carrier efficacy based on different covalent bond modifications for cancer treatment and introduces a novel drug delivery system that synergistically combines imaging and targeting capabilities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer treatments commonly include surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation therapy. However, once the tumor metastasizes, therapeutic intervention becomes more challenging [1]. While chemotherapy is a widely used and effective strategy to manage advanced cancer, conventional methods have significant drawbacks. Specifically: (1) They struggle to discriminate between healthy and cancerous cells, resulting in non-specific cytotoxicity. (2) The therapeutic efficiency is suboptimal, and high drug dosages can lead to severe or unpredictable side effects. (3) These drugs often display poor structural stability, causing cell damage due to issues like static electricity-induced cell extravasation. (4) Many cytotoxic drugs have undesirable pharmacokinetic profiles, such as a short half-life, necessitating frequent administration [2]. In recent decades, the emergence of self-assembled anti-cancer nanosystems as a subset of nanomedicine has garnered significant interest. Their facile synthesis processes are particularly noteworthy [3]. Researchers are now harnessing nanoparticles for targeted drug delivery, tissue-specific targeting, use as contrast imaging agents, and diagnostics. These nanoparticles encompass a range of materials, including metal nanoparticles, carbon-based nanomaterials, liposomes, and polymers [3–9].

In the quest to develop an effective drug delivery platform, nanocarbon materials have emerged as a promising choice. Among the various types of nanocarbon materials, Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have garnered significant attention due to their inherent advantages in drug delivery systems [10]. These advantages include surface functionalization capabilities, high drug loading capacity, and distinctive photoluminescence [11–14]. As a result, GQDs have the potential to serve as multifunctional carriers. Notably, they are predominantly utilized for carrying the cancer drug doxorubicin (DOX). Yang et al. highlighted that when the concentration of doxorubicin is at 0.47 mg/ml, the loading capacity of GQDs can reach up to 2.35 mg of doxorubicin per mg of GQD. The primary mechanisms underlying this drug loading are hydrophobic-hydrophobic interactions, π-π stacking interactions, and Van der Waals forces. This sheds light on why drugs with benzene rings, such as doxorubicin, interact efficiently with GQDs. The release mechanism of doxorubicin is intriguing. The –NH2 group on doxorubicin transforms into NH3 + in an acidic environment, which increases the drug's hydrophilicity and prompts its release from the GQDs. This mechanism ensures that doxorubicin is not indiscriminately released; instead, its release is favored in acidic environments, such as those predominant in cancer cells, thereby enhancing its specificity towards cancer cells [12,15]. The consistent superior performance of GQDs underpins their potential to enhance composite drug delivery systems and ensure efficacious therapeutic outcomes.

Another promising material, Barium titanate, has been extensively studied for its piezoelectric properties owing to its high dielectric constant, compressive strength, and porosity. Recently, the focus on barium titanate nanoparticles (BTNPs) has shifted towards their potential in biomedical applications, especially as components of delivery systems [16]. This shift is attributed to their tunable size and notable biocompatibility. For instance, Chiofani et al. investigated the capability of BTNPs to adsorb and subsequently deliver the drug doxorubicin to human neuroblastoma cells, SHSY5Y [17]. Interestingly, the drug and BTNPs interacted through a self-assembly mechanism driven by hydrophobic interactions rather than covalent coupling. Other research indicates that BTNP can be employed for gene delivery, achieving enhanced cellular uptake when coated with polymers [18]. Additionally, BTNP's unique properties make them promising imaging probes suitable for second harmonic imaging and micro-computerized tomography (micro CT), leveraging their nonlinear optical and high-density attributes [19–21]. Such reports underscore the potential of BTNP in pioneering advanced drug delivery systems.

Motivated by the aforementioned research, this study seeks to integrate barium titanate nanoparticles (BTNPs) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using covalent bonding, constructing a multifunctional acid-triggered drug delivery system for the targeted inhibition of cancer cells. Upon loading with the anti-cancer drug doxorubicin, the system is further encapsulated with hyaluronic acid (HA) [22,23]. This encapsulation not only optimizes the pharmacokinetics but also significantly boosts the system's water dispersibility. Intriguingly, although numerous studies have documented the various surface modifications and applications of GQD composites, there has been limited comparative analysis of their biomedical applications under different bonding conditions. To address this gap, we synthesized the composites via two distinct covalent bonding pathways and evaluated their efficacy in inhibiting cancer cells, as illustrated in Scheme 1 a. Furthermore, the differential effects of these surface bonding methods were elucidated using xenograft animal models, and robust mechanistic insights were gathered, offering valuable perspectives for the application of such composites in oncological contexts.

Hydrogen peroxide (aqueous solution 35 %). Barium titanate(IV) (nanopowder < 100 nm, purity > 99 %), hydrochloric acid (1 N), and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES, purity ≥ 98 %) were purchased from Sigma Chemical Co. GO quantum dots (GQD, purity > 80 %, C:1mg/mL, size: <15 nm) were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was purchased from Genetex. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)-propyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Hyaluronic acid (MW: 5 ∼ 150 kDa) was purchased from TCI. Ethanol (purity 95 %) was purchased from Echo Chemical Co.(Taiwan) Ultrapure water was employed in all experiments in this study. All chemicals were used without any further purification.

Studies have shown that thermal treatment of H2O2 in aqueous solutions of ceramic oxides leads to the decomposition of H2O2 and hydroxylation of the ceramic oxide surface. To hydroxylate, BaTiO3 nanoparticles were refluxed in an aqueous solution of H2O2 at 85 °C for 8 h. The H2O2-pretreated BaTiO3 (BTNPs-OH) was filtered from the suspension without any further heat treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQD@BTNPs composites via esterification
ce:section.ce:para: First, BTNPs-OH powders were dispersed in de-ionized water and mixed with an aqueous solution of carboxylic-GQD under sonication at 5 W to form a homogeneous solution. Then, 100 µL of sulfuric acid was slowly added to the reactant. The esterification process was implemented under a preheated 85 °C oil bath with vigorous magnetic stirring for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained suspension was dialyzed using dialysis membrane tubing with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Da in water for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQD@BTNPs composites via amidation
ce:section.ce:para: We functionalized the surface of BaTiO3 nanocrystals with amine groups and further conjugated GQD on the surface. To fabricate amine-functionalized BTNPs, a 200 mL suspension of BTNPs-OH powders was mixed with 200 µL APTES in ethanol and water. The mixture was transferred into the reflux system with magnetic stirring under oil-bathing at 70 °C. After 24 h, BTNPs-NH2 were filtrated through 0.22 µm filter membranes and washed with ethanol several times. Then, the procedure for preparing GQD@BTNPs(amide) was described as follows: First, 2 mg EDC and 1.2 mg NHS were dissolved in de-ionized water, respectively. This was followed by the addition of the EDC aqueous solution to a stirred suspension of the aqueous solution of carboxyl-GQD in de-ionized water, and the NHS aqueous solution was subsequently added to the solution for 5 min under magnetic stirring. Afterward, BTNPs-NH2 powders were dissolved in the resulting solution at room temperature for another 24 h to obtain the GQD@BTNPs (amide) suspension. The suspension was dialyzed using dialysis membrane tubing with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Da in water for 24 h.

Drug loading was initiated by separately dispersing 1 mg each of GQD@BTNPs (ester) and GQD@BTNPs (amide) in a PBS solution (pH 7.0) containing 1 mg DOX. This was achieved by ultrasonic vibration for 1 h, followed by stirring for 24 h at room temperature in the dark. The DOX-loaded GQD@BTNPs were then dialyzed using a dialysis membrane tubing with PBS to remove any unbound DOX. Concurrently, the Drug Loading Capacity (DLC) and Drug Loading Efficiency (DLE) were determined. The DLC and DLE calculations were based on ultraviolet (UV)–visible (Vis) spectrophotometry at 480 nm:
(1)
L o a d i n g
C a p a c i t y
( % )
=
C i
- C
r
Cs
× 100 %
(2)
L o a d i n g
E f f i c i e n c y
( % )
=
C i
- C
r
C i
×
100 %
Ci and Cr are initial and residual DOX molecules concentrations, respectively. Cs are the concentration of sample (HA-GQD@BTNPs).

2 mg of HA was dissolved in the previously prepared DOX-loaded BTNPs-GQD solution (DOX-GQD@BTNPs) and dispersed by ultrasonic vibration for 1 h. Then, the solution was mixed for 24 h under magnetic stirring at room temperature in the dark. Finally, the HA-coated DOX-GQD@BTNPs were dialyzed using dialysis membrane tubing with PBS to remove the free HA, resulting in the HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs suspension.

The morphology and structure of the as-prepared nanoparticles were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (HITACHI S-4800), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100; JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The crystal phase was evaluated with an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) on a Philips X’Pert MPD instrument. A UV−Vis spectrophotometer on HITACHI U-5100 instrument was applied to determine the characteristic absorption of the as-prepared samples. Zeta potential was detected by a Zetasizer Nano ZS Nanosizer (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were obtained with a Jasco FTIR-4600 spectrometer. FTIR spectra were obtained using an attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory.

Human colon cancer HCT116 cells (ATCC) were cultured in RPMI medium (Gibco) supplemented with 5–10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco) and 1 % antibiotic solution (Gibco). The cells were incubated at 37 °C in incubators with a 5 % CO2 atmosphere.

Approximately 6 × 103 cells were seeded into each well of a 96-well plate and allowed to adhere for 24 h. After this, cells were treated with either free DOX or compounds from the sample group for a specified duration, followed by analysis using the MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay (Gold brand). Briefly, after the treatment period, MTT reagent was added to each well, and plates were incubated at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere for 4 h. The medium with MTT was then carefully removed from each well, and DMSO (Dimethyl sulfoxide) was added to solubilize the formed formazan crystals. The resulting purple solution's absorbance was measured at 570 nm using an Epoch Microplate Spectrophotometer. Cell morphology was inspected using the IX73 Inverted Microscope. Photographic records of cell morphologies at varying drug concentrations were taken under both white light and fluorescence illumination at 100× magnification.

Six-week-old nude mice were subcutaneously injected with 1 × 106 HCT116 cells into the right leg, and were randomly grouped with N ≥ 3 in each group. The mice's body weight and tumor size were measured three times a week. When the tumor size reached 100 mm3 on day 12, 1.5 mg/kg DOX or 100 μl 10-fold HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs dilutions were administered to the mice by intraperitoneal injection three times a week for four weeks. At the end of the experiment, the tumors were collected and measured, and mean ± SEM was used for body weight and tumor size analysis. On the 35th day of the experiment, the HA-DOX-GQD@BTNP (amide) group received an intraperitoneal drug injection, followed by transfer to a micron-scale computed tomography imaging system (Micro CT Bruker 1176) for contrast agent image analysis.

A series of tests explore physical and chemical properties for the fabrication of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs. As revealed in Fig. 1 a, the figure displays the XRD analysis of barium titanate and GQD composite materials. The XRD pattern analysis, aligning with JCPDS no. #892475, unveils the cubic phase of BaTiO3. The most pronounced peak is observed at 2θ = 32.17° (110). Other peaks at 2θ = 22.208° (100), 32.17° (110), 38.898° (111), 45.590° (200), 50.812° (210), 56.127° (211), 65.779° (220), 70.323° (300), 74.788° (310), and 79.011° (311) authenticate the emergence of perovskite barium titanate [24]. Additionally, the peaks of GQD@BTNPs (illustrated in brown and blue) align precisely with the standard BTNPs (depicted in black), confirming that the BaTiO3 nanoparticles retain their original structure post modification with GQD, irrespective of the process being esterification or amidation. This underscores that the GQD modification does not alter the BaTiO3 lattice structure. After dialyzing the sample to remove excess GQDs from the synthesis, the UV–Visible absorption spectrum confirmed the successful formation of GQD and BTNP composites, as depicted in Fig. 1b. Compared to pure BTNPs, both GQD@BTNPs (amide and ester forms) exhibited a distinct characteristic absorption peak in the 220–250 nm range [13,25]. This typical peak is attributed to the π–π* transitions of graphite's sp2 hybridized domains [26,27]. Furthermore, the successful surface functionalization and conjugation of GQD@BTNPs composites have been corroborated through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), with the FT-IR spectra of BTNPs-OH, BTNPs-NH2, and GQD@BTNPs showcased in Fig. 1b. Initially, the hydroxylation result is evident, with the O−H bond stretching portrayed as a peak in the 3900–2800 cm−1 spectral region. A minor peak is discerned in GQD@BTNPs (amide) and BTNPs-NH2 at 1150 cm−1, attributed to the Si–O–Si silanol bond originating from APTES. This might be due to the double silanol grafting reaction observed in amination [28]. Conversely, the spectra of the GQD@BTNPs nanoparticles reveal a new stretching vibration of –CONH– at 1,574 cm−1 and 1,648 cm−1, signifying the interaction between the carboxyl groups of GQDs and the amino groups of BTNPs-NH2 [29]. Remarkably, GQD@BTNPs (ester) manifests a distinctive peak at 1114 cm−1, indicating an ester linkage between GQD and BTNPs [30]. In brief, the comprehensive FT-IR results ascertain that GQD is affixed to the BTNPs surface through the refluxed esterification or amidation of –COOH and –OH/NH2 groups present on GQD and BTNPs. However, the linkage of the amide and ester adduct in BTNPs-GQD could not be discerned in the FT-IR spectra due to the overlapping broad O−H peak. Hence, ζ-potential analysis was employed to further investigate the linking groups and the surface species of GQD@BTNPs-based nanomaterials, aiming to enhance their dispersibility, functionalization, and conjunction of particles. The ζ-potential of BTNPs was around 0 mV, which altered to +24.8 mV post surface silanization to the amine-terminal (Fig. 1c), confirming successful functionalization with APTES. Post esterification and amidation with GQD, a value of −14.02 mV was recorded for GQD@BTNPs. Lastly, upon coating GQD@BTNPs with hyaluronic acid (HA), the ζ potential shifted from −14.02 mV for GQD@BTNPs to −24.6 mV for HA- GQD@BTNPs, driven by the highly negative charge of the carboxyl groups. This shift denotes the successful modification of HA nanoparticles. Thermal stability is discussed using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), as shown in Fig. 2 . The comparison between the two groups of materials clearly highlights differences in thermal stability. Both groups exhibited degradation of surface residual functional groups around 170–200 °C and 290–320 °C [31,32]. The main degradation temperature for GQD was observed at 420–450 °C [33]. Notably, the amide-modified GQD@BTNPs group showed degradation of the surface silane, applied in the APTES step, around 550 °C [33].

Elemental analysis was employed to elucidate the presence of the GQD@BTNPs composite. Fig. 2a–c and Table 1 presents the Energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDS) patterns of BTNPs and GQD@BTNPs composites to ascertain whether GQD has been modified on the surface of BTNPs. Fig. 3 a depicts that pristine BTNPs materials comprise elements Ba, Ti, O, and C. In the table shown in Table 1, the signal of the C element in pristine BTNPs is presumably attributed to the detection of carbon tape. Moreover, Fig. 3b and 2c demonstrate that the carbon signal intensity of GQD@BTNPs (ester) and GQD@BTNPs (amide) is significantly elevated compared to that of pristine BTNPs, showing an increase from 3.44 % to 21.71 % and 20.94 %, respectively. This suggests that GQD is indeed modified on the surface of BTNPs. The element ratio indicates that BTNPs have been successfully functionalized with APTES during the amidation step, as evidenced by the silicon signal detected. Additionally, morphology of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs are illustrated in the Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images and size distribution (DLS measurements) depicted in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a and b exhibit the different bonded HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs (amidation and esterification) respectively. Broadly, there isn't a substantial variance in appearance between the two systems, and their size range from about 100 to 200 nm. It's apparent that a layer of hydrogel-like material envelops the nanoparticles, embodying the HA form, which aligns with our anticipations [34–36]. This setup thoroughly decorates the barium titanate nanoparticles with GQDs, enhancing water stability via the HA coating. To provide a more detailed view of the components, compositions without HA (GQD@BTNPs) are presented in TEM images in Fig. 4c and d. Without HA, the morphology of barium titanate nanoparticles is clearly visible, particularly in GQD@BTNPs(amide), where a layer of silane formed by APTES modification can be observed on the surface. In GQD@BTNPs(ester), GQD particles attached to the surface are distinctly visible. Further high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 4e) demonstrate the distribution of GQDs within the barium titanate nanoparticles. The presence of numerous light black dots, representing GQDs, distributed throughout the cubic crystal structure of the barium titanate nanoparticles indicates the success of our designed composite process. To assess the overall size distribution, Fig. 4f presents the results of Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) tests performed with a zetasizer on the system (HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs) in PBS. The data indicate that both systems exhibit a size distribution approximately between 200 and 400 nm, with peak positions at 276 nm (amide) and 312 nm (ester). The results for size distribution demonstrate good dispersion with slight aggregation. This is consistent with our observations from the comprehensive TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug loading and releasing

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 illustrates the cytotoxicity study results of free DOX, HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(amide), and HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(ester) against HCT-116 cells, evaluated by the MTT assay following a 24, 48 and 72-hour incubation period. Cell viability across these systems was also assessed against a control (untreated) group. As depicted in Fig. 5a, each group exhibited a good dose-dependence, with their cancer cell eradication performance being comparable to or surpassing that of the free doxorubicin group. Fig. 5c delineates the cancer cell killing efficacy post 72-hour co-incubation with cancer cells, underscoring the benefits of the drug delivery system in terms of prolonged release and enhanced cellular uptake. Therefore, the calculation of the IC50 (half maximal inhibitory concentration) illustrates the notable potential of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNP in inhibiting cancer cells. Intriguingly, the amide bond group exhibits significantly greater efficacy in cancer cell inhibition compared to the ester bond group. A plausible explanation for this may be found in previous studies, which suggest that BTNPs can selectively release free radicals to cancer cells [42]. However, the free radical scavenging properties of GQDs are well-documented, attributed to the resonance structure of their π orbitals [43–46]. This creates an antagonistic mechanism, where the variance in the strength of the electron transfer mediated through covalent bonding becomes apparent. Prior research in our research group indicates that in the ester group, electron withdrawal by carboxylate groups enhances electron transfer from BTNP to the GQD surface, potentially promoting radical adduct formation or electron transfer to GQDs [47]. Conversely, in the amide group, the amine functions as an electron-donating group, offsetting the electron affinity of the carboxylate group [30]. Hence, besides the inherent cytotoxicity of doxorubicin, BTNP offers additional cancer cell inhibition capabilities. The ester bond mitigates the additional oxidative stress from BTNP due to electron transfer, resulting a consequently diminished cancer cell inhibitory effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo xenograft animal model experiment.
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the promising anti-cancer potential of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs demonstrated in in vitro experiments, we proceeded to evaluate their efficacy in a murine model. Nude mice with implanted tumors were administered with injections of PBS, free DOX, HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(amide), and HA-DOX-GQD@BTNPs(ester) aqueous solutions, to assess the impact of this drug delivery system on tumor treatment. As depicted in Fig. 7 , the study spanned a duration of 35 days. Throughout the treatment period, both the body weight of the mice and the size of the tumors were meticulously monitored. In comparison to untreated mice, those administered with free DOX and carriers loaded with doxorubicin exhibited a substantial inhibition of tumor growth, with the amide group rendering significantly better inhibitory effects than the ester group. Such advancements are anticipated based on in vitro experiments, wherein sustained drug delivery facilitates the accumulation of the drug within tumors, thereby underscoring the influence of varying bonding groups. This observation was further corroborated by the visual examination of the tumors. Additionally, there was negligible variation in body weight among most groups, highlighting the excellent biosafety of this drug delivery system. Interestingly, HA-DOX-GQD@BTNP functions as a multifaceted drug delivery system and shows promise as a contrast agent in computed tomography, owing to the high density of barium titanate nanoparticles [21]. A Supplementary video vividly illustrates the presence of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNP in the intestines and stomach post intraperitoneal injection. Sequential scanning reveals numerous high-contrast white spots distributed throughout the intestines, affirming its efficacy as an imaging agent. This substantial body of evidence not only underscores the effectiveness of HA-DOX-GQD@BTNP as an innovative multifunctional drug delivery system but also elucidates the distinctions among various bonding types in biological models.

In this study, barium titanate nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots were successfully conjugated via two covalent bonds (amide and ester), creating effective nanoparticles for targeted chemotherapy with potential as contrast agents. The HA-DOX-GQD@BTNP formulation developed herein exhibits distinct advantages in terms of efficient drug loading, pH-sensitive drug release, and sustained release properties. Its efficacy in inhibiting cancer cells was distinctly observed in both in vitro and in vivo experiments. Notably, the two covalent bonding pathways resulted in marked differences in cancer cell inhibition. Potential explanations for these differences have been proposed, focusing on bonding affinity and electron transfer, aligning closely with the experimental outcomes. Overall, this study not only advanced an effective multifunctional carrier but also offered novel insights into the application of different covalent bonds in cancer therapeutics.

Jung-Hua Lin: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Li Chen: Formal analysis, Investigation. Er-Chieh Cho: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Kuen-Chan Lee: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was partly supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC 112-2628-B-038-009-MY3, 112-2221-E-152-001), Taiwan. We also appreciate the supports from the Core Facility Center and the Animal Center at Taipei Medical University and National Taipei University of Education (NTUE), Taiwan, for the instrumental analysis and technical support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2024.100713.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
